items.title: Westside had 200 stores in FY22, 300 stores in FY26; 97 cities and 7.26 mn sq feet area Zudio had 233 stores in Fy22, 963 stores in FY26; 313 cities a...
items.description_html:
Westside had 200 stores in FY22, 300 stores in FY26; 97 cities and 7.26 mn sq feet area

Zudio had 233 stores in Fy22, 963 stores in FY26; 313 cities and 10.32 mn sq feet area

Both brands were at the same store count in FY22 but today, Zudio is 3.2x of Westside's store count

That’s the power of Mass brands in India, Zudio is on fire and the same is evident by store additions

Also, the sq feet area per store is much higher in Westside v/s Zudio , as westside is premium and Zudio is below rs 1000 product brand

No stock reco

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Two really interesting results in Aerospace and Power theme: 1. GE Aerospace: Demand continues to be strong for commercial E...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Two really interesting results in Aerospace and Power theme:

1. GE Aerospace: Demand continues to be strong for commercial Engines

GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) reported better-than-expected first quarter financial results, driven by sharp growth in its commercial engines and services business and a significant increase in orders.

Total orders for the quarter reached $23 billion, an 87% increase from the prior year period, supported by demand for commercial engine services, which rose 39%.

2. GE Vernova: Gas Turbines and AI fuelled power demand

Power unit's core profit rose 57% to $811 million in the first quarter, while the electrification unit's profit more than doubled to $528 million.

GE Vernova also expects higher gas power revenue in the second half of the ⁠year as it ships more turbines and ramps up capacity to nearly 20 GW

The company added $13 billion to its backlog, taking the total to $163 ‌billion. It now ⁠expects the backlog to reach $200 billion by 2027, a year earlier than its previous forecast.

Multiple proxies to these trends are available in India

items.title: RT CSEINDIA: 📢 Episode 2 of our Solar Villages series is out! A ground report from the heart of rural Jharkhand. In Jharkhand’s Gumla district, a ...
items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
📢 Episode 2 of our Solar Villages series is out! A ground report from the heart of rural Jharkhand.

In Jharkhand’s Gumla district, a quiet revolution is underway. In Patratoli village, women have taken charge of a solar mini-grid, ensuring reliable electricity for their community. They are called Solar Vali Didis. Solar didis are trained to run, manage, and maintain clean energy systems.

This episode tells the story of the Rani Urja Mandal, a women-led collective that is not just powering homes, but also enabling livelihoods, safety, and independence. From reducing dependence on diesel to lighting up streets and small businesses, the impact is visible across the village.

Through the voices of women like Alma, Jyoti and Susila, we explore how decentralized renewable energy is transforming rural life, both socially and economically. Watch the episode now on @down2earthindia's Youtube channel : https://youtu.be/R-Ej839qg8M

items.description_html:
RT Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas #MoPNG
FAKE NEWS

There are some news reports suggesting a price hike of petrol and diesel. It is hereby clarified that there is no such proposal under consideration by the Government.
Such news items are designed to create fear and panic amongst the citizens and are mischievous and misleading.

In fact, India is the only country where petrol and diesel prices haven’t increased in the last 4 years. Govt of India and Oil PSUs have taken relentless steps in order to insulate the Indian citizens from steep increases in international prices.

items.description_html:
While the world’s attention has moved to stablecoins, India is quietly chipping away at its CBDC.

At least 10 pilot programs are underway to route welfare payments - climate aid to food subsidies - via CBDC.

At the location of one such pilot, farmers said it helped them avoid upfront payments, merchants said it has improved demand, and officials said it helped in better targeting.

A super story by @kalrajs23 and @AshwinM_

Read here: https://www.reuters.com/world/india/india-digital-currency-push-targets-its-leaky-welfare-system-2026-04-23/

items.title: 🚨Episode 2 of @down2earthindia’s ‘Solar Villages’ series is out! A ground report from the heart of rural Jharkhand. In Jharkhand’s Gumla distri...
items.description_html:
🚨Episode 2 of @down2earthindia’s ‘Solar Villages’ series is out! A ground report from the heart of rural Jharkhand.

In Jharkhand’s Gumla district, a quiet revolution is underway. In Patratoli village, women have taken charge of a solar mini grid, ensuring reliable electricity for their community. They are called Solar Vali Didis. Solar didis are trained to run, manage, and maintain clean energy systems.

This episode tells the story of the Rani Urja Mandal, a women-led collective that is not just powering homes, but also enabling livelihoods, safety, and independence. From reducing dependence on diesel to lighting up streets and small businesses, the impact is visible across the village.

Through the voices of women like Alma, Jyoti and Susila, we explore how decentralized renewable energy is transforming rural life, both socially and economically.

Watch episode: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R-Ej839qg8M

items.title: Taiwan's stock market just made history: The total market cap of Taiwan's stock market is up to $4.14 trillion, surpassing the UK's $4.09 trillion for...
items.description_html:
Taiwan's stock market just made history:

The total market cap of Taiwan's stock market is up to $4.14 trillion, surpassing the UK's $4.09 trillion for the first time.

Taiwan's market cap has TRIPLED since 2020, driven almost entirely by the AI-fueled surge in semiconductor stocks.

Over this period, Taiwan Semiconductor, $TSM, which makes up more than 40% of Taiwan's total market value, has surged +680% and is trading near its all-time high.

By comparison, the UK's market remains roughly in-line with its 2013 peak and pre-Financial Crisis highs.

Meanwhile, foreign investors purchased +$8.9 billion of Taiwanese shares so far in April, on track for the largest monthly inflow on record.

AI is reshaping the global stock market.

items.title: Super Interesting - China runs one of the most generous R&D tax incentive regimes in the world. As of 2026, nearly 85% of government support for busin...
items.description_html:
Super Interesting -

China runs one of the most generous R&D tax incentive regimes in the world. As of 2026, nearly 85% of government support for business R&D flows through tax incentives.

Companies can claim a 200% pre-tax deduction on eligible R&D expenses.

Machine tool and semiconductor manufacturers receive an even higher 220% deduction until 2027.

Eligible costs include researcher salaries, materials, prototype design, testing, depreciation & maintenance. Businesses are, indeed, leveraging and benefiting from state-sponsored support

Every dollar a Chinese firm spends on innovation costs them less than fifty cents.

Innovation isn't an option anymore, it's a necessity!

items.title: The gap between older and younger equity holders has never been wider: Americans aged 70+ now hold a record 17% of all US equities and mutual fund sha...
items.description_html:
The gap between older and younger equity holders has never been wider:

Americans aged 70+ now hold a record 17% of all US equities and mutual fund shares outstanding.

The percentage has TRIPLED since the 2008 Financial Crisis low.

At the same time, Americans under 40 own just ~3%, in-line with 2003 levels.

This percentage is HALF of what it was in the late 1980s.

As a result, the difference between the two cohorts is now ~14 percentage points, the widest on record.

By comparison, the gap stood at ~6 percentage points at the start of the 1990s.

The wealth gap is widening.

items.title: Risk appetite in South Korea is skyrocketing: Margin loans outstanding jumped to a record $23 billion in South Korea. Margin debt has DOUBLED over the...
items.description_html:
Risk appetite in South Korea is skyrocketing:

Margin loans outstanding jumped to a record $23 billion in South Korea.

Margin debt has DOUBLED over the last year and is now +37% above the 1-year average of $17 billion.

As a result, the KOSPI index has rallied +27% since the April 1st low and is up +52% year-to-date.

This has been driven by chip stocks, Samsung and SK Hynix, which are up +69% and +81% so far this year.

Both names together now account for ~40% of the Korean index.

Retail investors in South Korea are piling back into equities with record leverage.

items.title: Japan's exports are ramping up despite the Iran War: Japan’s exports jumped +11.7% YoY in March, the 2nd-highest since May 2024. This marks a sharp a...
items.description_html:
Japan's exports are ramping up despite the Iran War:

Japan’s exports jumped +11.7% YoY in March, the 2nd-highest since May 2024.

This marks a sharp acceleration from the +4.0% increase in February.

Exports to China drove the surge, rising +17.7%, led by chips, electronic components, and industrial metals.

At the same time, imports soared +10.9% YoY, widening the trade surplus to ~$4.2 billion, the highest since December 2020.

Meanwhile, crude oil imports fell -7.3% in value terms, as tankers that had already departed before the war continued arriving through mid-March.

China’s demand is driving the Japanese economy.

items.title: *Havells India Ltd Q4FY26 Concall Update* (Nirmal Bang Retail Research) *#Overall weighted average price growth across categories can be estimated in ...
items.description_html:
*Havells India Ltd Q4FY26 Concall Update*
(Nirmal Bang Retail Research)

*#Overall weighted average price growth across categories can be estimated in the range of 5-10%*
*#Major capex for FY27 & FY28 is allocated to Cables & Wires (C&W) and a R&D Centre*

*Outlook: Long-term Positive*

*Key Highlights*
•Overall quarter performance was modest with just 2.4% yoy revenue growth and margins at gross / EBITDA level both declined by ~70bps yoy.
•There was an impact on cooling products inventory due to mild start to summer season. Industrial infra demand remained strong, but consumer categories experienced challenges amid inflationary pressures from global disruptions.
•Company’s A&P spends surged 24% yoy / 14% yoy for higher visibility. Also, it recognized one-time gain of Rs. 283 cr on account of its unrealized gain on the investment in Goldi Solar Pvt Ltd in Q1FY26. The same has led to 40%+ growth in its reported PAT. Adj PAT excluding one-time gain reported a decline of ~15% yoy at Rs. 440 Cr.
•Cables performed well with sustained growth in cables while wires saw a muted performance. C&W overall volume growth stood at 6% yoy. Segment margins were supported by rising copper prices; however, pricing growth was estimated at ~8% yoy, that shows company’s calibrated approach in pricing actions to maintain the market share, growth and margins.
•Lighting revenues posted a stable growth of ~2% yoy, as LED lighting prices are stabilizing. The segment contribution margin for the quarter stood ~37% which is led by a one off like year-end releases for the entire year and it expects long term average remains in the range of 30-32% at normalized level.
•ECD revenues (mainly fans and air coolers) impacted due to delayed summer and unseasonal rains.
•In others category, there was a strong scale up in renewables space. Havells is focusing on adding more solar capacity and aspire to take the advantage of tailwinds of current demand scenario. Upcoming year expects lot more opportunities to drive more penetration and gain volumes. Better product additions to support the margins while focusing on gaining share in such competitive market.
•Lloyd business moderated due to previous year strong base and delay in summers. Margins impacted due to lower revenues. It has witnessed higher inventory in the 1st half of April month due to slower sales, and likely to be normalized by the end of this month. It can expect increase in the prices in 8%-15% range.
•Switchgears margins impacted due to lag in passing of cost; expected to normalize in coming quarters.
•Cooling products like Acs, fans and air coolers have seen price increase during Q4FY26 led by implementation of new BEE norms and further increase is expected on the back of increase in RM prices in the near to medium term while maintaining balance in the market share and margins.
Stock is trading at P/E of 38x FY28E EPS


Yatin Mota: Havells results below estimates

Need to check on inventory gains.

That could have led to beat in Wire and Cables business

items.title: BREAKING: Crypto funds attracted +$1.4 billion in inflows last week, the highest weekly inflow since the 3rd week of January. This marks the 7th weekl...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Crypto funds attracted +$1.4 billion in inflows last week, the highest weekly inflow since the 3rd week of January.

This marks the 7th weekly inflow out of the last 8, the strongest streak since October.

Bitcoin funds led with +$1.1 billion, bringing year-to-date total to +$3.1 billion.

At the same time, Ethereum funds attracted +$328 million, their strongest weekly intake since January, bringing year-to-date total to +$197 million.

Inflows were equivalent to 0.91% of total assets under management (AUM) last week, the highest weekly proportion year-to-date.

As a result, the total AUM of crypto funds surged to $155 billion.

Risk appetite in crypto is rapidly returning.

items.description_html:
BREAKING: US margin debt dropped -$32 billion in March, to $1.22 trillion, the lowest since November 2025.

This marks the 2nd consecutive monthly decline, totaling -$59 billion.

However, margin debt is up +$341 billion YoY, or +39%, a rate of growth last seen during the 2021 meme stock frenzy.

Since the 2022 bear market low, margin debt has surged +$570 billion, or +87%.

Over the same period, the S&P 500 has risen +97%.

The market is now highly reliant on leverage.

items.title: When Ashok Damani jokingly said to his father, Ramesh Damani, that he had met the NBCC management more than he had met his fiancée! Ramesh Damani Sir...
items.description_html:
When Ashok Damani jokingly said to his father, Ramesh Damani, that he had met the NBCC management more than he had met his fiancée!

Ramesh Damani Sir's words - "On average, I meet one company a month. When I am researching a company, I initially meet them often. In my mature investments, I don’t meet.

Let me tell you an interesting story. My son Ashok was getting into the business back then. I used to send him out to visit companies. I was buying NBCC in a big way at that time.

He had been to Delhi three to four times, and I used to send him to meet NBCC each time to find out what was going on. Then he got engaged. When he was going to Delhi, I asked him to meet NBCC.

He said that he has met NBCC more than he has met his fiancée! When NBCC got listed, I used to meet them once every two months, but now it’s on autopilot. I haven’t met them in three years now."

A Funny story indeed!

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: Trent delivered strong Q4 with 19.2% revenue growth and 32.6% PAT growth, though results were in-line with preliminary guidance. EBITDA margin expande...
items.description_html:
Trent delivered strong Q4 with 19.2% revenue growth and 32.6% PAT growth, though results were in-line with preliminary guidance. EBITDA margin expanded 171 bps YoY to 18.1% with robust cash conversion (1.55x CFO/PAT).

The company added 250 stores in FY26 while maintaining operating leverage (employee costs +3.6% vs revenue +17.2%). Key concerns are the 95.5% decline in JV/associate profits and higher D&A from expansion. Management's confidence is evident in the ₹6 dividend, 1:2 bonus issue, and ₹2,500 Cr equity raise authorization.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 19.2% YoY to ₹5,027.99 Cr in Q4, 17.2% for FY26 to ₹20,074.21 Cr

PAT grew 32.6% YoY to ₹413.10 Cr in Q4, 12.2% for FY26 to ₹1,721.33 Cr

EBITDA margin expanded 171 bps YoY to 18.1% in Q4, 102 bps for FY26 to 17.5%

Operating cash flow was ₹2,667.62 Cr with CFO/PAT ratio of 1.55x indicating high-quality earnings

Free cash flow was ₹904.77 Cr despite ₹1,762.85 Cr capex on store expansion

Added 250 stores in FY26 (total 1,286 stores), with Westside at 300 and Zudio at 963

Employee costs grew only 3.6% YoY vs 17.2% revenue growth, showing technology-driven operating leverage

Announced ₹6 dividend per share, 1:2 bonus issue, and ₹2,500 Cr equity raise

Risk Factors

Associates/JV profit share declined 95.5% YoY from ₹86.50 Cr to ₹3.90 Cr, dragging consolidated PBT

Depreciation grew 52.1% YoY and finance costs 21.5% YoY, faster than revenue due to IndAS 116 and new stores

Operating EBIT margin compressed 16 bps to 11.7% for FY26 due to higher D&A from expansion

Consolidated PAT growth (11.2%) lags Standalone PAT growth (24.1%) due to JV/associate drag

items.title: Tata Communications delivered a solid Q4 with 9.4% revenue growth driven by Data Services (11.4% YoY). The company achieved its Net Debt/EBITDA target...
items.description_html:
Tata Communications delivered a solid Q4 with 9.4% revenue growth driven by Data Services (11.4% YoY). The company achieved its Net Debt/EBITDA target of sub-2x at 1.99x, and the Digital Portfolio crossed the 50% revenue threshold for the first time - a key inflection point.

However, reported PAT declined 75.1% YoY due to a high base from exceptional gains, and normalized PAT fell 14.3% YoY due to elevated tax rates. The margin target of 23-25% by FY28 remains distant given the current 19.4% margin.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 9.4% YoY to ₹6,554.15 crore

Data Services revenue grew 11.4% YoY with 38% EBIT growth

Digital Portfolio crossed 50% threshold at 51.2% of Data revenue

EBITDA margin expanded 86 bps YoY to 19.6%

Net Debt/EBITDA achieved 1.99x target (sub-2x)

Free cash flow more than doubled to ₹2,045.92 crore

Operating cash flow/PAT conversion strong at 4.47x

Debt/Equity improved from 3.60x to 2.78x

Risk Factors

PAT declined 75.1% YoY due to high base from exceptional gains in Q4 FY25

Normalized PAT declined 14.3% YoY due to higher tax rate (42.2%)

Voice Solutions EBIT fell 51.2% YoY with margin compression to 4.6%

Subsidiaries contribute 70% of revenue but only 21% of PAT - margin drag

Network costs grew 13.6% YoY outpacing revenue growth (9.4%)

Contingent liability of ₹7,513.71 crore related to DOT AGR demands

items.title: SANGAMIND delivered strong earnings growth with PAT up 160% YoY and margin expansion of 369 bps, meeting revenue and ROCE guidance. However, the compa...
items.description_html:
SANGAMIND delivered strong earnings growth with PAT up 160% YoY and margin expansion of 369 bps, meeting revenue and ROCE guidance. However, the company missed its EBITDA margin target and critically failed its deleveraging commitment with debt rising ₹141 Cr versus the planned reduction.

Working capital deterioration and cash conversion weakness are concerns despite the operational turnaround. The capex cycle completion positions the company for improved cash flows, but balance sheet stress requires monitoring.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 13.2% YoY to ₹3,234.5 Cr, meeting management's guidance of 12-15% growth

PAT surged 159.8% YoY to ₹8,260 Lakhs in FY26

EBITDA margin expanded 369 bps YoY to 11.3% in Q4

ROCE improved to 12.1% in FY26, meeting management's 12% target

PBT before exceptional items grew 135% YoY for full year FY26

Capex cycle completed with CWIP declining 52.7% YoY

Risk Factors

Debt increased by ₹141 Cr in FY26 versus target to reduce by ₹350 Cr over 3 years

Operating cash flow declined 38% YoY to ₹26,872 Lakhs despite strong PAT growth

Trade receivables grew 23.1% YoY, outpacing revenue growth of 13.2%

EBITDA margin of 9.7% for FY26 missed 12% target by 230 bps

Current borrowings surged 34.8% YoY, indicating liquidity stress

Subsidiary contributed -3.8% to consolidated PAT, a drag on profitability

items.title: Havells Q4 FY26 delivered mixed results with reported PAT up 40% YoY driven entirely by a one-time ₹282.74 Cr fair value gain on Goldi Solar investme...
items.description_html:
Havells Q4 FY26 delivered mixed results with reported PAT up 40% YoY driven entirely by a one-time ₹282.74 Cr fair value gain on Goldi Solar investment. Excluding this, normalized PAT declined 3.4% YoY reflecting weak underlying operational performance. Cables segment was a standout with 35.9% EBIT growth and 230 bps margin expansion, while the solar business is progressing well towards its ₹1,000-1,500 Cr target.

However, Lloyd Consumer and ECD segments faced significant headwinds with revenue declines and margin compression. Channel inventory remains elevated despite management's guidance for normalization. The company's strong cash conversion (0.93x CFO/PAT) and continued capacity expansion (₹1,484 Cr capex) provide some strategic upside, but near-term growth remains muted.

Positive Factors

Normalized EBITDA grew 46.4% YoY to ₹1,025.75 Cr (excluding ₹282.74 Cr fair value gain)

Cables segment EBIT grew 35.9% YoY with 230 bps margin expansion to 14.2%

Solar business (Others) grew 48.8% YoY to ₹1,726.93 Cr, on track for ₹1,000-1,500 Cr target

CFO/PAT conversion strong at 0.93x with FCF of ₹87.54 Cr despite ₹1,484 Cr capex

Lighting & Fixtures margins expanded 490 bps to 21.3% with improved product mix

Dividend increased to ₹10/share (₹6 final + ₹4 interim)

Risk Factors

Lloyd Consumer revenue declined 18.8% YoY with EBIT loss of ₹27.16 Cr

Electrical Consumer Durables revenue declined 2.2% YoY with 220 bps margin compression

Switchgear margins at 23.2% significantly missed 37-40% guidance range

Channel inventory remains elevated with 9.8% YoY growth vs 3.4% revenue growth

Normalized PAT declined 3.4% YoY excluding one-time fair value gain

Revenue growth muted at 2.5% YoY in Q4

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: Lunchtime humour - In a factory, a man was standing on the floor and looking aimlessly. The CEO of that factory came and asked h...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
Lunchtime humour - In a factory, a man was standing on the floor and looking aimlessly. The CEO of that factory came and asked his salary.

Man replied - "Rs.5000 sir".

The CEO took out his wallet and gave Rs.15000 and told him "I pay people here to work and not to waste time, This is your 3 months salary. Now get out of here. Never come back".

That guy left. Then CEO asked workers "Who was that guy?"

Workers replied "Pizza delivery Boy, sir".

Moral: Don't overreact in every situation! 😀😛😂

items.title: I just finished reading Sorkin's "1929". It doesn't provide anything new in the way of analysis or explanation of the events of the late 1920s and ear...
items.description_html:
I just finished reading Sorkin's "1929". It doesn't provide anything new in the way of analysis or explanation of the events of the late 1920s and early 1930s, but it is a lot of fun to read, has a huge cast of primary and secondary (and tertiary) characters, and provides a great deal of color on well-known figures like Richard and George Whitney, Charlie Mitchell, William Durant, Carter Glass, and at least a dozen others.

Later today I will be meeting a Chinese student who is going off to Yale in September to study economics and history, and I plan to give him my copy of this book as an ideal way for someone unfamiliar with the history to start learning about one of the most important periods in modern economic history.

items.title: The recent surge in commodity chemical stocks appears largely cyclical, driven by temporary supply side disruptions rather than any meaningful structu...
items.description_html:
The recent surge in commodity chemical stocks appears largely cyclical, driven by temporary supply side disruptions rather than any meaningful structural shift. I remain unconvinced that current geopolitical dynamics are creating a lasting change in the industry’s fundamentals.

There may be isolated, product specific tailwinds, but the broad based recovery across commodity chemical players, from NOCIL to Deepak Nitrite, Ingrevia and DMCC, suggests a cyclical rebound rather than a structural re rating. If this were truly structural, the divergence would likely be more selective and sustained, rather than this synchronised upswing.

That said, it is worth considering whether second order geopolitical effects could gradually reshape supply chains. For now, however, the evidence points more towards cyclical normalisation than a paradigm shift.

items.title: RT Aaron Levie: If you read this and don’t understand why it’s happening it’s an opportunity to reset your understanding of how the real world work...
items.description_html:
RT Aaron Levie
If you read this and don’t understand why it’s happening it’s an opportunity to reset your understanding of how the real world works.

The real world will need a ton of help actually getting agents going in the enterprise. Companies have legacy tech stacks they need to modernize, data in tons of fragmented tools, knowledge that isn’t captured or digitized, and change management needed to actually utilize agents effectively. And they have to do all this while still running their business day-to-day, unlike startups.

This is why there is so much opportunity for companies (software or services) to actually deploy agents in specific domains and workflows. This remains a big opportunity for both existing services providers but also tons of new startups as well. Every new technology wave produces a new era of consulting firms that can deliver on that technology.

It’s also why the FDE model is going to be alive and well for a long time because companies will want to have their vendor actually help drive the change management and implementation for their new workflows.

The people aren’t going away. Far from it.


First Squawk: OPENAI WORKING WITH CONSULTING FIRMS, INCLUDING ACCENTURE, CAPGEMINI AND PWC, TO HELP SELL CODEX TO BUSINESSES- WSJ

items.title: Central banks are sitting on massive stockpiles of gold after years of record purchases: Global central banks now hold ~38,666 tons of gold, reflectin...
items.description_html:
Central banks are sitting on massive stockpiles of gold after years of record purchases:

Global central banks now hold ~38,666 tons of gold, reflecting ~17% of all gold ever mined.

Still, the largest portion of gold remains in private hands, with ~97,645 tons held as jewelry, or ~43% of the total.

Investment-related holdings, including bars, coins, and ETFs are ~50,978 tons, making up 23% of the total.

The remaining ~32,602 tons, or 14%, fall into other categories, such as industrial use and private reserves.

Central banks are now major players in the global gold market.

items.title: The global debt crisis is set to get even worse: Total sovereign and corporate bond issuance is estimated to rise to a record $28.8 trillion in 2026. ...
items.description_html:
The global debt crisis is set to get even worse:

Total sovereign and corporate bond issuance is estimated to rise to a record $28.8 trillion in 2026.

That would mark the 4th consecutive annual increase and would also DOUBLE the average pre-pandemic levels.

Corporate debt issuance is set to surge to a record $6.9 trillion, while government debt issuance is expected to rise to $21.9 trillion, also an all-time high.

By comparison, governments and corporates issued $23.7 trillion of debt in 2020, during the pandemic.

As a percentage of GDP, global issuance is expected to increase to 23.3%, the 2nd-highest on record, only behind the 27.5% peak during the pandemic in 2020.

To put this into perspective, the 2008 Financial Crisis peak was 21.4%.

The world is borrowing ABOVE crisis levels.

items.title: RT Michael McNair: Blanchard dismisses the argument that global imbalances create financial risks, bc he says what matters is gross positions, not net...
items.description_html:
RT Michael McNair
Blanchard dismisses the argument that global imbalances create financial risks, bc he says what matters is gross positions, not net. But the fact that financial instability can exist without persistent imbalances doesn’t mean persistent imbalances don't cause it. Just like lung cancer can exist without smoking but that doesn’t mean smoking is at most a marginal contributor to lung cancer. In fact, the causal chain from persistent imbalances to financial instability was mapped out decades ago.

Joan Robinson's beggar-thy-neighbor argument showed that a surplus country can relieve its own demand deficiency by exporting it abroad so that the deficit country is forced to absorb another country's domestic imbalance. When one group of countries runs persistent surpluses and refuses to rebalance by raising domestic demand, some other group of countries has to run the mirror image deficits and absorb the shortfall in global demand.

Keynes explained that the deficit country can respond to these beggar-thy-neighbor policies by either accepting lost output through higher unemployment and deflation, or they can do it by expanding credit and public deficits to sustain demand. Either way, persistent imbalances shift the pressure of adjustment onto balance sheets. That was the entire logic behind his insistence at Bretton Woods that surplus countries had to face pressure to adjust too. If only deficit countries are forced to adjust, you get either economic contraction or rising leverage.

Kindleberger then showed how the capital flows associated with persistent imbalances impact financial markets and build the structures through which instability eventually emerges. Large inflows into a country that doesn't need external savings compress yields, lower risk premia, encourage speculation, and push credit into weaker uses. His work is filled with historical cases where external surpluses and deficits fed lending booms, speculative manias, and bad debt cycles.

Minsky then explained the balance sheet mechanism through which net imbalances create financial instability. If a country accommodates persistent demand leakage through debt growth rather than immediate contraction, the financial system changes character over time. Units that begin in hedge finance move into speculative finance and then Ponzi finance. So persistent external deficits that are accommodated through credit expansion create the kind of environment in which leverage rises, asset prices are bid up, and borrowers become increasingly dependent on rollover conditions staying benign.

Jacques Rueff adds another important piece by pointing out that reserve currency systems allow deficit financing and external adjustment to be postponed far longer than under a harder monetary regime. That delay is destabilizing bc it lets the stock of claims and the underlying distortions grow much larger before it results in a crisis. In a gold system the adjustment is brutal but faster. In a reserve currency system, adjustment can be deferred. While that might look stabilizing for a while, it often means the eventual instability is bigger bc the imbalances have had more time to reshape the credit system and asset markets.
Bernanke acknowledged this structural flaw, stating that the international monetary system "lacks a mechanism, market based or otherwise, to induce needed adjustments by surplus countries, which can result in persistent imbalances." And that “global rebalancing of trade and current accounts, are an essential outcome for long run economic and financial stability.”

The bottom line is that persistent imbalances create financial instability bc a system that forces one set of countries to absorb the counterpart deficits will eventually make them do so through unemployment, debt, or asset bubbles, and that’s exactly how financial fragility builds.


Olivier Blanchard: The argument in the G7 report https://shorturl.at/zZf25 that global imbalances create financial risks is unconvincing.
Suppose there were no imbalances, either in stocks (assets= liabilities) or in flows (zero financial account). The risk that some investors decide to sell would

items.title: RT Richard Wu: The structure of the deal is pretty interesting here. I think what’s happening is: 1. xAI is having trouble training a SOTA coding mod...
items.description_html:
RT Richard Wu
The structure of the deal is pretty interesting here. I think what’s happening is:

1. xAI is having trouble training a SOTA coding model (hence cofounder departures), bunch of idle GPUs
2. Cursor doesn’t have capital to blow on a $5B training run to compete with Codex/Claude
3. xAI says to Cursor: use all the GPUs you want at cost and get to a SOTA coding model, as long as we have an option to buy you
4. Cursor also gets a free option: train a model better than Opus and get bought out for $60B, or get $10B that pays for all the GPUs you rented

Win win

items.title: RT Ian Miles Cheong: Marc Andreessen just revealed the Elon Musk philosophy that completely broke his brain: "The best product in the world shouldn't ...
items.description_html:
RT Ian Miles Cheong
Marc Andreessen just revealed the Elon Musk philosophy that completely broke his brain: "The best product in the world shouldn't even need a logo."

We all know Elon is relentless about quality. As Marc puts it: "Do you want the best car in the world or not, right? Like that's Elon's mentality... And it's working very well."

But at a recent event, Elon took this mindset to a completely different level. He dropped a perspective so jarring that Marc initially thought it was a joke.

Elon’s thesis? "You shouldn't even have to have your name on the product. It's just obvious. Everybody knows."

The logic is brutal but simple. If you build the undeniable, undisputed best thing in the world, everybody uses it. And because everybody uses it, you don't need to slap your branding all over it to prove it's yours.

Think about that. We spend endless hours agonizing over marketing, tweaking brand colors, and putting our logos on every square inch of what we build. But the ultimate flex isn't a flashy logo. The ultimate flex is building something so undeniably brilliant that its mere existence is the brand.

Strong results from PNB Housing - more importantly Commentary is positive🟩

This is creating positive rub off across the Housing finance sector (mainly affordable housing finance)

- Loan book expected to cross 1 Lakh Cr in FY27 - overall growth guidance 18-20%

- Retail loan book projected to grow 18-20% - growth to be driven mainly by affordable & emerging segments

- Affordable segment expected to grow fastest - management guiding ~50% growth in disbursements

- Affordable + emerging mix currently at 40% - expected to move towards ~50% over next 2 to 3 years supporting yield improvement & margin expansion

- Management indicated yields have likely bottomed out - expected to improve with higher mix of emerging, affordable and corporate segments

- ROA guidance at 2.4-2.5% for FY27 supported by mix improvement & recoveries

- Credit cost expected to remain negative again in FY27 (~15 to 30 bps benefit) due to recovery from legacy pool

- GNPA improved below 1% (0.93%) - asset quality remains healthy

Management clearly shifting portfolio mix toward affordable & emerging segments due to higher growth visibility

items.description_html:
I always focus on mapping the cycle, every business is at some or the other stage of the business cycle , most of the game is figuring out how much differential we are willing to pay for that current cycle to peak cycle transition in the context of the current multiple

That's why lot of times we don't buy the best company in the sector but still may have a superior return profile, there are no fixed rules but focus on understanding this

items.title: As a country gets richer and simultaneously ages, deaths from communicable diseases and conditions related to childbirth become more preventable and l...
items.description_html:
As a country gets richer and simultaneously ages, deaths from communicable diseases and conditions related to childbirth become more preventable and less common. Consequently, the relative share of deaths from non-communicable diseases such as heart disease and cancer begins to rise.

India is in the midst of this epidemiological transition. Non-communicable diseases account for over half of all deaths in India as of 2022, the latest year for which this data is available.

Among non-communicable diseases, heart disease is the largest contributor to Indian mortality and is now responsible for one in three deaths. From middle age onwards as the relative risk from infectious diseases declines, age and factors including obesity and hypertension put people at greater risk.

Cancer kills over 6% of Indians every year, with a higher contribution to mortality among those in their late 40s and early 50s in particular. 15% of deaths of women aged 30-54 are from cancer.

What are the top causes of death from communicable diseases in India? Read @Rukmini's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/indias-disease-transition/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Diseases #Health #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: For those who are interested in Japan's recent economic history, and what that may tell us about other economies that copied its growth model, it is w...
items.description_html:
For those who are interested in Japan's recent economic history, and what that may tell us about other economies that copied its growth model, it is worth noting that in Japan, the term “excess competition” (過当競争, katō kyōsō) emerged, mostly in the 1960s, to express what was seen at the time as an urgent problem.

Japanese officials used it to mean competition so intense that it wiped out profitability, discouraged private investment, and created instability in key industries. It also led to financial fragility because of the associated dependence of Japanese companies on unlimited access to easy credit to stay alive.

items.title: Mr Ramesh Damani - "The way you do investing is - you keep a circle of competence and do your homework, don’t get complacent and lazy, which we tend ...
items.description_html:
Mr Ramesh Damani -

"The way you do investing is - you keep a circle of competence and do your homework, don’t get complacent and lazy, which we tend to be after some time.

If you are generally a smart person, then there will be tons and tons of opportunity. Here is the thing that RK Damani told me. I used to ask him in the 1990s, after he had made tons of money and I hadn’t . I said “you people are lucky that you bought Indian stocks in the 1980s. Aap logon ka paisa bangaya. Mein abhi aaya hoon. Mera Nahi Banega” (“You people have made money. I have just come. I won’t make money.”)

He said, “Ramesh, I am telling you this today. The opportunity in the next 20 years will be far greater than those in the past 20 years. Trust me on that.” He has been proven absolutely correct.

In December 2016, there was a conference in Kolkata. Someone asked him the same question and he answered the same - “The best time to invest in India is today. The opportunity of the last 30 years will be dwarfed by the opportunity of the next 30 years.” I also truly believe that.

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: RT CNBC-TV18: ‘India and China had the same per capita GDP in 1990. Today, China is 5X ahead.’ So what went wrong? On Young Turks Reloaded, Co-found...
items.description_html:
RT CNBC-TV18
‘India and China had the same per capita GDP in 1990. Today, China is 5X ahead.’

So what went wrong?

On Young Turks Reloaded, Co-founder of TeamLease and Co-author of Made In India, Manish Sabharwal delivers a blunt reality check -
‘The most embarrassing number about India? 35 years after 1991, 45% of our labour force still works on farms.’

But here’s the twist - Manufacturing alone won’t fix it. ‘We will never have 28%-45% of our workforce in factories.’

The real battleground? ‘We don’t have a shortage of land, labour or capital, we have a problem in how they combine.’

And it starts early:
📚 Nearly half of India’s children depend on government schools - where quality remains deeply inconsistent
🌏 In contrast, only 5% in Japan, 10% in the UK and 15% in the US go to private schools - because public education works

‘If anything should be free and high-quality, it is school education.’

The takeaway:
China didn’t win because of communism, it won because it unleashed entrepreneurship.
India’s path? Fix education. Unlock entrepreneurs.

Catch the full show - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4q3cc9HNPyc&t=265s

@ShereenBhan

items.title: RT Spharaka Networks: This weekend, a multi-billion dollar tech company was breached. Not through malware. Not through a zero-day. Not through phishin...
items.description_html:
RT Spharaka Networks
This weekend, a multi-billion dollar tech company was breached.

Not through malware. Not through a zero-day. Not through phishing.

Through an AI tool that had OAuth access to an employee's workspace.

The attacker compromised a third-party AI application, inherited its trusted permissions, pivoted into internal systems, and accessed secrets and deployment pipelines, all without writing a single exploit.

This is the new kill chain. And most enterprises are completely unprepared for it.

Every AI agent connected to your email, documents, code repositories, or cloud infrastructure is a privileged insider operating at machine speed. When that agent is compromised, the attacker doesn't need your password. They already have your trust.

The numbers should concern every security leader:

→ 490% increase in AI-related attacks year over year
→ 48% of security professionals rank agentic AI as the #1 attack vector of 2026
→ 86% of organisations have zero visibility into where their AI tools are connected
→ Over half of deployed AI agents operate without any security monitoring

This is the threat model we built Spharaka Sphere™ to defend against, autonomous, AI-native defence that monitors identity chains, detects trust anomalies, and responds at machine speed before human analysts even see an alert.

In a world where attackers use AI agents, defenders need autonomous AI agents too.

The next breach won't come through your firewall. It will come through an integration you already approved.

#CyberSecurity #AIAgents #OAuthSecurity #AttackSurface #SaaSSecurity #IdentitySecurity #SupplyChainRisk #AutonomousDefence #SpharakaNetworks #SpharakaSphere #AuraXP #AgenticAI #CISO #EnterpriseSecurity #InfoSec

https://open.substack.com/pub/spharaka/p/the-trusted-agent-how-ai-copilots?utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web

Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) and Mishra Dhatu Nigam Limited (MIDHANI) are partnering to indigenize critical jet engine components, reducing reliance on foreign imports. items.title: Finally some good progress on Market Coupling - Round Robin system is no more - Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party - Gr...
items.description_html:
Finally some good progress on Market Coupling

- Round Robin system is no more
- Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party
- Grid India has 6 months to come up with the Market coupling algorithm similar to Europe's Day Ahead Market
- Post 6 months, then after everyone's approval they have to build the systems, the infrastructure, cybersecurity protection etc etc

Looks like this should take 12 to 18months to see some value

This is assuming IEX does not put new cases on the order to stall

Now contrary to popular belief - Market Coupling like the EU is actually good

- EU trading Market boomed post market Coupling
- The industry leader EPEX Spot who was a market leader before coupling still has most of the volume
- EU has only 2 players taking all the market share with Nord Pool being 2nd

IEX
- Yes market coupling without round Robin is good, but market coupling itself will take some market share
- At best market share should dip from 99% to 75%.
- At best post market coupling they would stall revenues for 1 year
- Post market coupling the regulator will boost Power trading a lot
- Volume will rise
- News instruments like CfD coming to power exchanges

Verdict
Short term IEX will be volatile
For long term investors the game will start post Market Coupling

Today Indias largest Power exchange is at close to a billion dollars.

Disc - Invested in IEX at Lower Levels. Not a buy or sell reco

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Nomura on Yes Bk Neutral, TP Rs 21 4Q26: Improving fundamentals; core-ROAs still catching up Broad-based strength across growth, margi...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Nomura on Yes Bk
Neutral, TP Rs 21
4Q26: Improving fundamentals; core-ROAs still catching up
Broad-based strength across growth, margins and profitability
ROA at 0.95%; SR recoveries to moderate from FY26 levels
Levers for profitability at play, but core-ROA profile build up would take time
Strategic execution of new management is a key monitorable

MS on Yes Bk
UW. TP Rs 15
Core PPOP was in line vs MSe.
PAT miss of 2% was due to Rs3.4bn one-off provisions.
Credit costs were stable, aided by recoveries.
NIM improved QoQ and was above MSe.
Balance sheet growth improved to 11% YoY (3% in F3Q)
Stay UW given valuation significantly above fundamentals.

JPM on Yes BK
UW, TP Rs 18
operating performance in
4QFY26 beat JPMe with PAT of Rs 10.7bn (+45% y/y, +12% q/q) coming 9% above JPMe, driven by
(i) cost control – opex rose by just 2% YoY, and came 2% lower vs JPMe.
(ii) CIR improved to 63%, but remains elevated vs peers and continues to weigh on RoA.
(iii) 28bps of credit costs booked during qtr were 4bps lower vs. JPMe
Yes Bank achieved an RoA of 1% for first time since reconstruction, however, supported by 31bps of contribution from SR recoveries
Believe that improving visibility on sustainability of these levels remains key, as risks from MSMEs are likely to emerge from supply chain disruptions, driving credit costs higher
Believe valuations at 1.2x P/B FY27E already price in much of b/s growth & RoA improvements.

@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: UBS on HDFC BK Buy, TP Rs 1175 Q4 PAT beat driven by lower provisions Loans grew c12% YoY; LDR down c400bp QoQ to 94.6% Management exp...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
UBS on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 1175
Q4 PAT beat driven by lower provisions
Loans grew c12% YoY; LDR down c400bp QoQ to 94.6%
Management expects loan growth momentum to sustain
Expect c14-15% ROE in FY27-28E

MS on HDFC BK
OW. TP Rs 1025
PAT beat MSe by 4% as sharply lower provisions and lower operating costs offset slightly lower core revenue (2% below MSe).
Commentary was anchored on loan growth with LDR no longer a constraint.
Earnings resilience and attractive valuation make it one of key picks amid geopolitical risks.

CLSA on HDFC BK
O-P, TP Rs 1200
4QFY26 NII and PPOP were largely in line, while PBT was a 4% beat
Would characterise it as a steady performance in a slightly challenging quarter.
Loan growth of 12% YoY was largely in line
Sequential quarterly-average (QA) deposit growth picked up from the past couple of quarters.
LDR (a metric we consider irrelevant, but many investors still find important) declined to 95% from 99% in prior quarter.
Margins were up 3bp QoQ, a decent outcome in this environment when cost of wholesale deposits rose sharply post the Gulf conflict.
HDFCB’s gross slippages have declined YoY on an absolute basis for three consecutive quarters.

JPM on HDFC BK
OW, TP Rs 990
Delivered a steady 4Q26 with PAT broadly in line vs JPMe.
While NII (-4% vs JPMe) and NIMs trailed JPMe on elevated competitive intensity and an adverse product mix, they were offset by lower than expected loan loss provisions.
NII growth for HDFCB has remained volatile, decelerating to 3.2% y/y in 4Q26 vs 4.8%/6.4% in 2Q/3Q, respectively
Expect NII growth to accelerate in FY27/28 on a better-than-expected pick-up in system credit growth and funding cost benefits from a decline in the share of higher cost borrowings.
Decline in average LCR to 114% (vs 116% in 3Q, 110-120% target range) does reduce balance-sheet flexibility to support margins/RoA in FY27

Bernstein on HDFC BK
O-P, TP Rs 1150
There was no dramatic improvement, but neither was there much to complain about, especially given b/s adjustment in progress.
While growth trends were largely known - slightly weaker loan growth & still healthy deposit growth driving an improvement in LDR - margins showed sequential resilience despite the decline in LDR
Earnings profile was supported by a sharp decline in credit costs & continued cost discipline, which helped offset moderation in non-interest income
As a result, bank delivered steady profitability, with RoA holding at 1.9% & EPS growth at 9% YoY (vs. consensus estimates of 6%).

Jefferies on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 1050
Q4 profit of Rs192bn, up 9% YoY, was in line with estimates.
Topline (NII & Fees) was slightly softer than estimates, but was compensated by operating synergies, lower credit costs & higher treasury gains.
Improved deposit growth of 14% aided loan growth of 12% and ~400bps QoQ fall in LDR.
Momentum in deposit growth & NIMs should be key drivers
Tweak estimates & see 15% CAGR in PBT (ex-treasury)

Nomura on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 950
Soft delivery on NIMs; lower credit costs drive PAT beat
Deposit growth keyamid tight liquidity buffers
Asset quality pristine; healthy loan and deposit growth
Leadership continuity and succession planning remain a key monitorable, with tenure of MD & CEO coming up for renewal (in Oct-26), alongside appointment of a new part-time chairman. Expect HDFCB to deliver RoA/RoE of 1.8%/14% over FY27-28F

Investec on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 915
A soft operational quarter in 4Q, with a subdued core PPOP performance (+3% YoY, 3% below est) driven by soft NII (+3% YoY) and fees (+6% YoY).
PAT rose 9% YoY to Rs 192bn, with RoA steady at 1.8% and broadly in line with estimates, aided by lower credit costs of 36 bps.
Loan growth remained steady at 12% YoY (+4% QoQ), while deposits grew strongly at 14% YoY (8.6% QoQ), lowering the CD ratio to 95%, with CASA deposits up 10% QoQ to 34.1%.
Deposit growth was aided by a strong periodend balance build up, as avg deposits/CASA growth was lower at 3.6% / 2.2% QoQ
LCR moderated further to 113.5% in 4Q (vs 116% as of 3Q).

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CITI on ICICI BK Buy, TP Rs 1720 4Q RoA of 2.4% surpassed CitiE, underpinned by near-zero credit cost (3bps), stable NIMs, and acceler...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CITI on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1720
4Q RoA of 2.4% surpassed CitiE, underpinned by near-zero credit cost (3bps), stable NIMs, and accelerated loan growth of 15.8% YoY/6.0% QoQ.
Positives
i) Broad-based growth momentum — led by rural (gold loans) and business banking, alongside retail (mortgages/PL/CV/CE) too stepping up; corporate portfolio expanded 3% QoQ.
ii) Elevated corporate recoveries, declining retail unsecured stress contained credit cost.
iii) 5bps IT refund interest lifted NIMs by 2bps.
Offsets:
i) Modest fee growth (7% YoY).
ii) Rs1.06bn treasury loss.
iii) Overhead costs rise (+14% YoY). iv) Deposits growth of 10.8% YoY/3.8% QoQ.
Outlook: Raise loan growth estimates to 15-16%, expect range-bound NIMs, agri-PSL provision reversal offers credit cost buffer.
Earnings estimates broadly retained — credit cost comfort, opex leverage, and improved growth offset by stable NIM and modest non-interest income.

CLSA on ICICI BK
O-P, TP Rs 1700
4QNII & PPOP were largely in line with estimates, while PBT was a 10% above due to negligible credit costs
Latter was driven by sharp recoveries from written-off accounts.
However, even excluding that, core asset quality was better than estimates
Key +ve was pickup in loan growth, up from 12% YoY last quarter to 16% YoY in 4QFY26
This should put to rest worries that ICICIB’s loan growth is slowing.
NIM was largely stable QoQ; a decent outcome in this environment.
Fee income remains sluggish as credit card spending is muted.
Lastly, asset quality continues to surprise positively with the gross/net slippage ratios improving 40bps/10bps YoY.

Nomura on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1620
4QFY26: Loan growth re-accelerates, credit costs surprise
Normalised PAT beat of 2%
Asset quality pristine; loan growth re-accelerates
With LCR remaining comfortable at 124%, BK is well positioned to defend near-term loan growth and NIMs
FY27-28F EPS are broadly unchanged, as lower credit costs assumptions are offset by softer fee income
Expect BK to deliver sector-leading 2.2% RoA & 16% RoE over FY27-28F.

Jefferies on ICICI Bk
Buy, TP Rs 1670
Q4 profit of Rs137bn, up 8% YoY, was ahead of estimates aided by significantly lower credit costs helped by some corporate recoveries and lower slippages in unsecured loans.
Pickup in loan growth to 16% is encouraging, aided by deposit growth of 11% and headroom in LDR now at 87% (83% last year)
See 13% CAGR in PBT (ex-treasury) over FY26-29E & ROE of 16% in FY27E.
Valuation at 2.2x FY27E adj PB is at deserved premium to peers.

Kotak Inst Eqt on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1800
Delivered 8% yoy earnings growth in 4QFY26 led by a sharp yoy decline in provisions.
Headline loan growth (16% yoy) and deposit growth (11% yoy) have improved, but operating profit growth was weak (3% yoy).
Asset quality indicators stayed stable.
As a sector leader, ICICI Bank has to deal with elevated expectations that are challenging to meet.
Combined with its valuation premium, this makes outperformance difficult
Like balance sheet resilience over growth.

Investec on ICICI BK
Buy, TP raised to Rs 1625
A strong Q4FY26, with PAT up 8% YoY and 21% QoQ to Rs 137bn (+6% ahead of est) with best-in-class RoA delivery of 2.4%.
Importantly, ICICI delivered strong NII growth (+8% YoY and 4.8% QoQ) with a sharp pickup in loan growth (+16% YoY vs 11.5% YoY as of 3Q) and stable NIMs (at 4.32%).
Loan growth was strong and broadbased at 16% YoY, led by business banking (+24% YoY) and rural loans (+26% YoY).
Core fee growth was however soft at 6% YoY, and higher opex capped core PPoP growth at 5% YoY.
Asset quality remained robust, with net slippages at just 35bp (annualized), and credit cost at just 3bp (vs 37bp in 3Q), helping drive 30bp QoQ RoA expansion

MS on ICICI Bk
OW TP Rs 1705
PAT beat MSe by 7%, largely driven by lower provisions.
Core PPOP was 1% above MSe, reflecting higher NII.
Adjusted NIM dipped 2bp QoQ to 4.27% (MSe 4.25%).
Loan growth accelerated to 16% vs. 10% in 2QF26.
Resilience with growth amid geopolitical uncertainties -

Bernstein on ICICI BK
O-P, TP Rs 1550
Q4 addressed key concern—loan growth—with bank delivering 16% YoY growth after several quarters of trailing system
However, deposit growth remained modest, particularly relative to system, possibly reflecting a conscious effort to protect margins
Overall, PPoP growth was somewhat subdued, weighed down by weak noninterest income & steady cost growth
A sharp decline in credit costs, however, provided a meaningful offset, supporting earnings growth.
As a result, profitability improved, with RoA expanding to 2.4% despite modest underlying operating trends.

JPM on ICICI BK
OW. TP Rs 1600
Delivered another quarter of industry leading growth with net advances up by 6% q/q, which came in better vs JPMe at 4.6% and vs large private banks at ~4% q/q
Thesis of positive inflection in NII growth was visible in 4Q as well with NII rising by 8.4% y/y vs 7.7%/7.4% over the last 2 quarters.
With probability of further rate cuts reducing significantly, expect NII growth to accelerate further to 12.3%/15.1% in FY28/28
Also, ICICIB affirmed no concerns in its loan portfolio (especially Business Banking which is 21% of advances) from the ongoing supply chain disruptions from the West Asia conflict, which is comforting

UBS on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1720
Q4 PAT beat driven by negligible provisions; Margins expand 2bp QoQ
Loan growth at c6% QoQ; LDR down 170bp
Bank confident on loan growth while NIMs to be range-bound
Est RoA of c2.2% (FY27-29E) for ICICI & expect most metrics to remain steady
STK trading at c2.x March 2027 PBV

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wa...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wave of companies. We learned a lot from the YC companies. We also made the conscious choice to pursue a different course.

In the ancient Bharatiya tradition of philosophical debate, I am going to offer this.

I will start with things I agree with YC on.

1. The biggest lesson anyone can learn from YC: small passionate teams can do magic. This was true way before AI coding arrived and will always be true.

2. YC is absolutely right to not over-emphasize "innovation". Doing a similar product as the bigger guys, faster and cheaper, is often the best course. Google did not invent search. OpenAI did not invent the LLM. Anthropic did not invent agentic coding.

3. YC companies tend to geographically cluster, and that can lead to subtle peer pressure and group-think. So the "laggard" founders who are not growing like a weed every week start to feel left out and eventually the result is "founder depression". And YC has counselors.

By the standards of 2007 silicon valley or even 2014 silicon valley, we were thought to be losers. Just keep that in mind.

4. Many deep tech problems like building a better, cheaper MRI machine or advanced semiconductor equipment require long focus and patient execution and lots of capital. These are endurance tests, not weekly sprints.

5. YC too often optimizes companies for "exit". That philosophy was built for and requires prolonged bubbles, which American policy has delivered, at the price of nearly wrecking the country (note the extreme inequality and political division). If you love India, you should not wish for similar bubbles.

6. YC model worked in silicon valley. One of the reasons it worked was that silicon valley could get any talent from anywhere in the world, notably from India, easily. That era may have ended or at least on pause right now.

Bengaluru has tried the same thing but with "any talent from anywhere in India" and we have not yet created huge companies. India needs its Huawei and Xiaomi and BYD and these companies are Chinese to the core, built by patriotic Chinese. Indian talent, staying in India, rooted in India, is going to have to build companies like them. Enough said.


Jared Friedman: What I told 2,000 future founders in Bengaluru today:

1/ We believe we are at the start of a second wave of Indian companies that will build world-class AI native products for the global market. Emergent and Giga are the model of the future.

items.title: Raamdeo Agrawal Sir on how his investing journey started – "During my CA course in Mumbai, I somehow got hooked onto the stock markets. I got to lear...
items.description_html:
Raamdeo Agrawal Sir on how his investing journey started –

"During my CA course in Mumbai, I somehow got hooked onto the stock markets. I got to learn about companies like Century Textiles, where I was doing the audits. Meanwhile, in 1980, my brother got into the merchant navy. He was making good money - around Rs.30,000-40,000 a month. He had saved about Rs. 4-5 lakh.

I suggested he invest that money in the stock market. I told him that I would help him invest. So that’s how I started my journey of investing in the stock markets in 1980, with my brother’s money. We went to the brokers for information, started reading accountancy books (which were not readily available), and international magazines like Fortune, which carried stock-oriented articles.

The good thing was that I was a habitual reader right from my childhood. As far as I can remember, I was a voracious reader. I used to go to the library almost every day and borrow whichever books I could lay my hands on, though it was mostly fiction.

After I completed my CA, I wondered what to do in life. The only thing that interested me was the stock market. I tried to practice as a CA for a year but realised pretty early that it was not my cup of tea. Then I decided to get into equity research. I

saw an advertisement for an equity research job in a company called ‘Aditi Investments’. It was like a God-sent offer for me. Can you believe that I was the only person who applied for that job? I got the job.

By that time, I had collected and read balance sheets for about 250 companies. My weekend job and passion were to collect balance sheets. I was so fascinated by everything. In the early 1980s, there were no databases or the internet. If I met somebody and got to know that his father invests in the stock market, I would make it a point to go to his house, make friends with his father, and gather all the balance sheets!

So, by 1985, I had read and internalised all the balance sheets by page. I had analysed them by capacity, location, utilization, cost structure, profit structure, etc. I became a moving encyclopaedia on that many stocks. Simultaneously, I was investing Rs. 5-6 lakh for my brother. I used to think I was a smart person. I did two years of formal equity research. I wrote a book, Corporate Numbers Game, in 1986

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: The real impact of AI is yet to come. The software business, as we knew it, is done for. But the physics aware AI led improvements can literally unloc...
items.description_html: The real impact of AI is yet to come. The software business, as we knew it, is done for. But the physics aware AI led improvements can literally unlock massive gains in the core industrial operations. With ~90% production line validations done with real data + AI simulators that use physics aware models, the efficiency gains will be massive. What's unfolding at Pepsico(with Siemens & NVIDIA) is going to set the narrative for the foreseeable future. Investory management, product mix, precise assortment planning, procurement & sales, pricing mix, all are set to transform. And, this play has many proxy plays that few are willing to look at.

items.description_html:
Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire accord agreed in Lebanon, while US President Donald Trump said he believed a deal to end the Iran war would come "soon," although the timing remains unclear.
Araghchi said in a post on X the Strait was open for all commercial vessels for the remainder of the U.S.-brokered 10-day truce between Israeli forces and Iran-backed Hezbollah agreed between Israel and Lebanon.
He said the passage of ships would need to be along the route that Iran's Ports and Maritime Organisation had announced.
@Reuters

items.title: Good way of looking at opportunity size, Case study by Ramesh Damani Sir - "It comes from an understanding of what you are investing in, rather than i...
items.description_html:
Good way of looking at opportunity size, Case study by Ramesh Damani Sir -

"It comes from an understanding of what you are investing in, rather than investing in the price action. That is the difference between fundamentalists and price action-oriented investors. An investor who is price action-oriented will think that the stock has tripled, so let me take some money home.

While the fundamentalist will understand that he has bought something that has deep value and even if the stock has tripled, it doesn’t mean that the value has been extracted.

Let me give you an example from my life to illustrate the point. In 2003-04, when I was trying to look for new themes, I realized that alcoholic beverage stocks were very cheap. You could have bought 50% of India’s liquor industry for a few hundred crores.

It’s staggering to think that - in a country with a 1 + billion population, with no religious inhibition about drinking, and where people are young and getting into drinking age. With Rs. 100 crore, you could have bought 10-20% in the Indian liquor industry or 2 flats in NCPA in Mumbai - that was the value proposition.

You could have seen what Anheuser-Busch is valued at in America and what San Miguel is valued at in the Philippines. Then if you bought with conviction, you would not sell even if it became Rs. 400 crore or Rs. 1000 crore, because that’s silly. It could be valued at Rs. 50,000 crore or Rs. 100,000 crore, or maybe there is no terminal value.

A true fundamentalist will figure that equation out, without knowing the exact timeframes. These just become a core part of the portfolio. That’s the difference between a true value investor, a fundamentalist, and a price action person.

That is where it is important to have a grounding in what you buy. I hung around with a group of people who were very deep-value investors, and that helped me immeasurably in life, because I have seen how they have done it. I have seen with my own eyes how Gillette shares were bought at Rs. 45. The share price went up to Rs. 100 and then Rs. 500, and not even 100 shares were sold, still sitting tight with it. It helps to have a good network."

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors; no stock reco

items.title: Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback? One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For com...
items.description_html:
Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback?

One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For comparison:

1 kg of coal → 3 kWh
1 kg of natural gas → 15 kWh
1 kg of diesel → 12 kWh

This is not a marginal improvement. It is a 50,000x advantage over coal by mass. It's why one standard 700 MWe PHWR in India replaces a 2,800 MW coal plant equivalent on annual energy.

items.title: While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households ...
items.description_html:
While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households that own a computer has increased from just over 1% to just over 9%. In most developed countries, by contrast, computer ownership exceeds 75%.

Among the 30 million households in India that own a computer, 90% have a laptop, while 20% own a desktop. A little under 10% of computer-owning households report having both a laptop and a desktop.

India has also made limited progress in the ability to use computers over the last decade. A little over one in five adults, or 200 million people, were able to operate a computer as of 2025. This share is roughly twice the share of households that own a computer, suggesting that many people may have learned to use a computer, or use one in college or at work, without owning one themselves.

While the proportion of rural adults who can use a computer has increased, it still stands at just about one in six. Among urban adults, the figure is much higher with over one in three adults, but it has grown only marginally over the last decade.

How does the ability to use computers vary by age group and gender in India? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/computers/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Computer #Technology #Laptop #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC). Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online ...
items.description_html:
Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC).

Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online retailers, as it allows them to hold inventory.

Eternal-owned Blinkit became one through a QIP, Swiggy is close to getting there, and Nykaa has always had this advantage.

Paytm is among the first major listed fintechs to get IOCC status.

There may not be immediate, direct business benefits, but this is important given the scrutiny Paytm has faced over ownership by China’s Alibaba and Ant Financial, which together held 38% before the IPO.

After the IPO in 2021, foreign investors held 81%, which has now come down to just below 50%.

items.description_html:
Updates on GLP 1 market in India

CareEdge Ratings estimates that India’s potential GLP‑1 market is set to expand nearly fivefold, from Rs 1,000–Rs 1,200 crore in 2025 to approximately Rs 4,500–5,000 crore by 2030.

Post patent expiry, drug prices are expected to decline by around 40–50% in FY27, followed by a further reduction of 10–30% in FY28 due to heightened competition.

Within 10 days of launch, generic semaglutide has added 40k new patients & already taken 24% value share of India’s sema mkt, with overall vols up 2.5X.

Eli Lilly's Mounjaro (tirzepatide), a competitor to semaglutide, became India's highest-selling drug by value and has overtaken Augmentin by GSK.

items.title: #LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that v...
items.description_html:
#LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that view to 3 years, and it still might be.

This gap comes down to time. As Howard Marks and Nassim Taleb remind us, risk and value shift with the clock. What feels expensive and fragile in the short run can turn reasonable once time allows the business to prove itself.

In 'Fooled by Randomness', Taleb tells a story about a retired dentist who is a skilled investor. The dentist expects to earn about 15% more than safe government bonds each year. But he checks his portfolio many times a day. This habit causes stress because, in the short term, prices move almost randomly. Taleb calls this “noise”, which simply means meaningless fluctuations that do not reflect real value.

Over very short periods, like minutes or days, the chance of seeing a gain is barely above 50%. This means that over three months, price movements are driven mostly by luck and maybe Q4 FY26 numbers or, rather, the interpretation of those. If a stock is already “not-so-cheap”, its price reflects a lot of optimism. There is little room for disappointment. Even a small negative surprise can push the price down. It's always albout the rate of change relative to the asking run-rate (valuations)

In 'Most Important Thing', Marks explains that risk often comes from paying too high a price. When you buy an expensive stock, you have almost no “margin for error”. This simply means you have little room to be wrong and still be okay. In the short term, bad luck or shifting sentiment can cause sharp falls. He also points out that being early can feel the same as being wrong, especially over short periods.

Taleb offers another example in 'Fooled by Randomness'. He describes the idea of building a ski resort in Miami. Imagine a rare blizzard hits, and the resort makes a huge profit in its first year. That success does not make it a good decision. It was simply luck. In the same way, making money on an expensive stock over 3 months may just mean you were lucky, not right.

Things look different over longer periods. As time passes, real business performance begins to matter more than daily price swings. Taleb notes that over a year, the odds of seeing gains improve greatly. Over 3 years, the company’s actual progress has a much better chance to show through the noise. What the business earns and builds starts to matter more than short-term sentiment.

Back in 2023, I was one of the few bullish voices on Laurus. Talk is cheap, so I backed that view with my own money. Sell ratings were common at the time, and many were anchored to targets around 250, which weighed heavily on sentiment.

Marks explains that while prices can drift in the short run, terminal value pulls them back over time. Terminal value is simply what a business is truly worth based on what it can earn and build.

For Laurus Labs, the CDMO business is that anchor. Over the next five to seven years, this segment alone could cross 10,000 Cr. in annual sales. Then, post-2033, the precision fermentation and bio-engine should be all guns blazing.

If a company grows well, it can “grow into” a high MCap. This is why a stock that looks expensive today may still work out over a few years. Over 3 years, a strong business may justify a high starting price. Over 3 months, you are mostly exposed to chance.

Taleb compares short-term risk to a version of Russian roulette with many chambers. You might avoid losses several times, but the risk is always there. Without a margin of safety, a sudden drop can happen at any time. This is why short-term investing in expensive stocks can feel unpredictable and fragile.

In the end, time changes the nature of the bet. Over 3 months, randomness dominates, and a high price leaves little protection. Over 3 years, business performance has time to come through, and value can catch up with price. The same stock can look risky or reasonable depending on how long you plan to hold it.

Laurus Labs is our core family holding, and none of this is an investment recommendation.

items.title: Technicals for power transmission space are turning back... The fundamental view point for the same:- India's power sector is entering a multi-year ex...
items.description_html:
Technicals for power transmission space are turning back...

The fundamental view point for the same:-

India's power sector is entering a multi-year expansion supercycle, shifting from an installed base of ~525 GW to over 800 GW. While the long-term narrative heavily favors renewables, near-term capital is flowing predominantly toward conventional thermal and transmission due to physical grid constraints and baseload realities

India ranks #3 globally in renewable additions (~45 GW added last year). The aggressive runway targets 50 GW/year over the next 5 years (~250 GW total). In contrast, conventional thermal power will add a steady 8-10 GW/year, led primarily by NTPC and Adani Power

The Unit Economics & Capex Math -

1) Solar: ~₹5 Cr/MW with a 1-2 year build time. India boasts a Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) of ~$30/MWh (₹2.5/unit), making it one of the cheapest globally due to high solar irradiance
2) Thermal (Coal): ~₹15 Cr/MW with a 5-year build time for greenfield projects
3) Nuclear: ~₹25 Cr/MW with a rigid 10-year build time. France serves as a historical parallel for India's nuclear ambitions, with long-term goals scaling from the current 8 GW to 100 GW by 2047

There is an under penetration in electrification currently, electricity comprises only ~18% of India's total energy mix, compared to ~30% in China and the low-20s globally. To achieve energy self-sufficiency amidst geopolitical volatility, power demand growth is projected to stabilize at a 1x multiplier to real GDP (~6% long-term growth)

Coming to a big tailwind of data centre capex, Current capacity sits at 1.5 GW, but is estimated to ramp up to 5-8 GW by 2030, driven by favorable grid access and clean energy mandates. This will incrementally consume ~2% of India’s overall electricity mix.

But what is the primary bottleneck with all this here? Transmission capacity… While India operates an efficient synchronous grid, a solar plant takes 1 year to build, whereas high-voltage transmission lines require ~3 years due to land acquisition and permitting. This lag will inevitably cap the realistic absorption of new solar capacity.

There might be a case where pure play renewable power generators might be in headwinds due to margin pressures and transmission bottlenecks which will make a investment case a little stronger for conventional power generators, power transmission value chain, new power infra such as nuclear energy and others and high quality/ top-tier EPC players.

Just quick note on few of the industry giants on this value chain -

1) NTPC - Operating as a highly defensive core holding. NTPC is leading the thermal capacity charge (17 GW under construction), spearheading future nuclear JVs, and incubating what will likely become India's largest renewable subsidiary

2) Power Grid - A direct beneficiary of the electrification and transmission theme, though near-term execution has been dragged by land acquisition hurdles. This one player drives compelete transmission value chain in the country

3) Larsen & Tubro - Largest EPC player present in multiple parts of the power value chain. The fundamental metrics are incredibly solid where ROEs are inching toward 17-19% with high-teens EPS visibility. However, the stock currently trades as a call on geopolitical stability, given that the Middle East accounts for a massive 37% of its order book.

4) PFC & REC - Their historical rerating was driven by the broad power theme, but loan book growth has fundamentally decelerated as Discom health improves and tier-1 banks chip away at market share. However, a potential merger between the two would be highly accretive—eliminating the ~50% holdco discount for PFC and forging a top-10 profitable Indian entity

5) Solar PV Manufacturers - Massive supply-side additions for solar cell manufacturing are coming online, which, coupled with transmission-capped demand, creates a high probability of margin compression over the next 12 months

6) IEX - Despite bottom-barrel valuations, structural and regulatory overhangs persist. Aside from the threat of market coupling, the exchange's transaction fees (~4 paise) are nearly 5x the global average (~1%), creating sustained headline risks

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

Custome index chart - https://www.stockscans.in/charts/PCI:TRANSMISSION (@stockscansin)

items.title: Organised manufacturing accounts for the majority of economic value added through manufacturing and employed 18.5 million people in over 200,000 facto...
items.description_html:
Organised manufacturing accounts for the majority of economic value added through manufacturing and employed 18.5 million people in over 200,000 factories in 2024.

The manufacture of food products employs the most Indian factory workers, accounting for over two million jobs. The manufacture of textiles and wearing apparel are other key sources of factory jobs, collectively employing over three million people.

The manufacture of food products is widely dispersed throughout the country, with Maharashtra being the leading employer. Tamil Nadu followed by Gujarat are India's textile manufacturing hubs. Tamil Nadu is India's apparel manufacturing hub, with more than one in three apparel factory workers in the state alone.

The manufacture of basic metals is largely confined to the mineral-rich states of Odisha, Chhattisgarh and West Bengal, as well as Maharashtra and Gujarat.

How much economic value does organised manufacturing create across different regions in India? Read Ishan Deodhar and @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/manufacturing-hubs/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Manufacturing #Industry #Economy #India #DataForIndia
